title: SSL certificate validation disabled — TrustAllStrategy in AlcdvServiceImpl HTTP client
description: AlcdvServiceImpl.getHttpClient() builds an Apache CloseableHttpClient with a trust-all SSLContext (TrustAllStrategy) and NoopHostnameVerifier. This silently accepts any SSL certificate, including self-signed or expired ones, making every outbound ALCDV call vulnerable to man-in-the-middle attacks. Code looks normal to a parser — the risk is only visible when you understand what TrustAllStrategy + NoopHostnameVerifier together mean at runtime.

title: Token passed as URL query parameter — visible in server logs, browser history, and proxies
description: AuthenticationResource.validateToken() accepts the token via @QueryParam("token"). Query parameters are written to every web server access log, stored in browser history, captured by reverse proxies, and included in HTTP Referer headers. Any production log file becomes a store of valid authentication tokens. A parser sees a valid @QueryParam annotation — it cannot know the parameter contains a credential.

title: JWT expiry not validated — expired tokens accepted indefinitely
description: AuthentiationServiceImpl (26 methods, 641+ lines) uses a custom TOKEN_DATE_FORMAT and DATE fields to build tokens. Reading the logic shows token expiry is checked by comparing a date string embedded in the token body — but the token signature is trusted from the hardcoded SECRET_KEY. Nothing enforces a maximum token age server-side. An attacker who captures one token has access forever. A static parser sees valid date-comparison code; it cannot infer that no expiry enforcement exists.
beforeCode: // Token contains date string; isEncryptedToken() checks format but never checks if date is in the past
afterCode:
// Use a standard JWT library (jjwt / nimbus-jose-jwt) with .setExpiration(new Date(now + TTL_MS))
// and verify with .parseClaimsJws(token) which automatically rejects expired tokens

title: Exception stack traces returned in API responses — internal structure exposed to clients
description: Multiple Resource classes (ApiResource, ConfigurationResource, ServicesHealthResouce) catch exceptions and return them directly in the response entity. Returning raw exception messages or stack traces reveals internal class names, package structure, SQL table names, and server paths to any client. This aids targeted exploitation. A parser sees valid try/catch blocks — it cannot read the intent of what is being returned inside catch.

title: No ownership check on consumer endpoints — any valid API key can read/update any consumer
description: GET /Consumer, POST /Consumerupdate, POST /Consumeradd, GET /Consumerdelete all validate that a request carries a known API key, but none verify that the caller is authorised to operate on the specific consumer record being requested. Any authenticated client can mutate records belonging to other clients by simply changing the consumer ID in the request. This is an IDOR (Insecure Direct Object Reference) — a parser only sees valid DB calls.
beforeCode:
// Repository method: consumer = repo.get(consumerId);
// No check that consumerId belongs to the calling API client

title: FileServiceImpl.processFile() (346 lines) silently swallows rows on parse error — data loss without indication
description: processFile() reads rows from an uploaded file inside a loop. When a row fails to parse (malformed column count, bad date format, etc.) the exception is caught per-row and the row is skipped. The API returns HTTP 200 with a generic success message regardless of how many rows were silently dropped. The caller has no way to know partial ingestion occurred. A parser sees ordinary try/catch inside a loop — the silent-skip behaviour is only visible by reading what happens in the catch block.

title: CorvetClientServiceImpl duplicate methods callToCovert / callToCovertWithSetTimeout — timeout only applied in one path
description: Two 63-line methods perform the same external HTTP call to the Corvet service. The only intended difference is a timeout. In practice both methods share most logic, meaning bug fixes applied to one are never applied to the other. More critically, callers that accidentally invoke callToCovert() instead of callToCovertWithSetTimeout() make unbounded-timeout calls to an external dependency under /health/corvetHealth. A parser sees two valid methods — it cannot understand the duplication is dangerous divergence.
beforeCode:
public Response callToCovert() { /* 63 lines */ }
public Response callToCovertWithSetTimeout() { /* 63 lines, timeout added */ }

title: CinPostgreSequenceGenerator.generate() called per-row inside batch — N database round-trips for sequence IDs
description: CinPostgreSequenceGenerator generates a sequence number by executing a SQL query (D6-001 already flags SQL injection there). Reading the call chain shows this generator is invoked for every row written during batch processing. For a file with 10,000 rows this adds 10,000 extra DB round-trips solely to fetch sequence IDs. A static parser found the SQL injection; it cannot trace the call frequency across the batch loop.
beforeCode: // Called once per row: String id = generator.generate(session, object);
afterCode:
// Pre-fetch a block of IDs in one query: SELECT nextval('seq') FROM generate_series(1, :batchSize)
// Cache block in memory; hand out IDs locally until block exhausted, then fetch next block

title: Authentication service returns different HTTP status codes for wrong password vs unknown user — user enumeration
description: When authentication fails, returning 401 for wrong-password and 404 for unknown user allows an attacker to enumerate valid usernames: try username — if 404 the user does not exist, if 401 the user exists but the password is wrong. All authentication failure paths should return the same 401 Unauthorized with the same generic message. A parser sees legitimate conditional status logic; it cannot understand the information-disclosure implication.
beforeCode:
if (user == null) return Response.status(404).entity("User not found").build();
if (!passwordMatch) return Response.status(401).entity("Invalid password").build();
afterCode:
// Same response for both cases — no distinction visible to caller
if (user == null || !passwordMatch) return Response.status(401).entity("Invalid credentials").build();

title: VIN lookup result cached in application-scoped object without invalidation — stale vehicle data served
description: AlcdvServiceImpl and the VIN-related resource classes store lookup results in instance or class-level fields of @Singleton-scoped resource beans. JAX-RS resources annotated @Singleton share one instance across all requests. A VIN detail fetched for request A is still in that field when request B arrives with a different VIN — if the null-check is missing or the assignment races, request B could receive request A's vehicle data. Static analysis sees correct field usage per request; it cannot detect cross-request contamination in singletons.
beforeCode:
private VinAlcdvDetails cachedResult; // instance field in @Singleton service
public VinAlcdvDetails getAlcdvAttributes(String vin) { cachedResult = repo.find(vin); return cachedResult; }
afterCode:
// Never store per-request data in instance fields of singleton beans
// Use local variables only: public VinAlcdvDetails getAlcdvAttributes(String vin) { return repo.find(vin); }

title: No structured error response format — clients cannot reliably parse error details
description: Across ApiResource, ConfigurationResource, LanguageResource, and ServicesHealthResouce, error responses return plain String entities with varying structures ('User not found', 'Error: ...', raw exception messages). A client consuming these APIs cannot write reliable error handling because the error shape is different per endpoint. This is an API design issue invisible to static analysis, which sees valid Response.status(...).entity(...) calls.
afterCode:
// Define one error DTO: { "code": "INVALID_API_KEY", "message": "..." }
return Response.status(401).entity(new ErrorResponse("INVALID_API_KEY", "Authentication required")).build();
